attributes.stem:
A 23 year old, G1P1, is seen on the ward round on day 1 post emergency Caesarean section for obstructed labour. The operation was complicated by a 1500ml blood loss and the post-operative orders include subcutaneous administration of 40mg enoxaparin daily for a total of ten days. The patient is requesting that the enoxaparin is ceased as she does not like needles.

She had a long latent phase and was immobile during labour with an epidural and syntocinon in the preceding 24 hours. She has a BMI of 40 and denies any history of previous VTE/PE however her mother had a DVT postpartum.
attributes.stemQuestion: During which one of the following stages of pregnancy is the risk of venous thromboembolism the greatest?
attributes.explanation:
Pregnancy is associated with a 4-5-fold increase in the risk of VTE compared to age-matched, non-pregnant women due to the increase in venous stasis, procoagulants and the pro-inflammatory effect of pregnancy on vessel walls. Whilst this is an uncommon complication, it is an important cause of direct maternal death.

The absolute risk of VTE is reported as 4.1 per 10,000 pregnancy years in the first trimester and 59.0 per 10, 000 pregnancy years in the third trimester. About 10% of DVTs that occur during pregnancy do so in the first and second trimesters, with about 28% occurring in the third trimester. However, the risk of VTE is highest in the postpartum period, 1-2 per 1,000 deliveries. Immobility is probably a contributory factor to this increased risk.

In counselling this patient, it is important that the risk of VTE and individual patient factors which are contributing to this recommendation for daily prophylactic thrombophrophylaxis is explained. Current management guidelines are based on formal VTE risk stratification and for this patient who has two risk factors (BMI>40 and Caesarean section in labour) the recommendation is for at least 10 days of prophylactic low-molecular weight heparin. This patient has also had a significant postpartum haemorrhage and is likely intravascularly dehydrated and will need to be encouraged to mobilise and have an adequate oral fluid intake.
learningPoints: The risk of pregancy-related venous thromboembolism is greatest in the puerperium.
references: Mclintock, C, Brighton, T, Chunilal, S, Dekker, G, McDonnell, N, Mcrae, S, Muller, P, Tran, H, Walters, BNJ & Young, L 2011, ‘Recommendations for the diagnosis and treatment of deep venous thrombosis and pulmonary embolism in pregnancy and the postpartum period’, Australian and New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology, vol. 52, no. 1.

Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Reducing the risk of venous thromboembolism during pregnancy and the puerperium. Green-top Guideline No 37a, April 2015. https://www.rcog.org.uk/globalassets/documents/guidelines/gtg-37a.pdf. Accessed 11/2/2021. Updated guideline can be found https://www.rcog.org.uk/guidance/browse-all-guidance/green-top-guidelines/thrombosis-and-embolism-during-pregnancy-and-the-puerperium-acute-management-green-top-guideline-no-37b/ (accessed 7th April 2023)
Jacobsen AF, Skjeldestad FE, Sandset PM. Incidence and risk patterns of venous thromboembolism in pregnancy and puerperium—a register-based case-control study. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2008;198:233.e1–7

Virkus RA, Løkkegaard EC, Bergholt T, Mogensen U, Langhoff- Roos J, Lidegaard Ø. Venous thromboembolism in pregnant and puerperal women in Denmark 1995–2005. A national cohort study. Thromb Haemost 2011;106:304–9

National Health and Medical Research Council. Clinical practice guideline of venous thromboembolism (deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism) in patients admitted to Australian hospitals. Melbourne: National Health and Medical Research Council; 2009. for the prevention
Dado CD, Levinson AT, Bourjelly G. Pregnancy and Pulmonary Embolism. ClinChestMed. 2018 September ; 39(3): 525–537
